title: Pimientos de Padrón
subtitle: Blistered Spanish peppers with sea salt
excerpt: Padrón peppers fried in hot olive oil until blistered and slightly charred, scattered with sea salt. Most are mild; a few are fiercely hot. Part of the pleasure is not knowing which.
sourceNotes: Pimientos de Padrón originate from Galicia, named after the town of Padrón near Santiago de Compostela. Documented in Penelope Casas, Tapas: The Little Dishes of Spain (1985) and Claudia Roden, The Food of Spain (2011).

content.text: The Galician saying is: unos pican y otros no, some bite and others don't. Early-season Padróns are almost all mild; peppers grown in summer heat develop capsaicin unpredictably, so roughly one in ten will be sharp enough to make you blink. This randomness is central to the appeal.

The technique is simple: very hot pan, good olive oil, whole peppers (uncut, unwashed, just dry), constant tossing until the skin blisters on all sides. The heat must be high enough to char the skin without steaming the inside. Seconds matter at the end. Once the salt goes on, they are ready to eat.

Dry the peppers completely, any surface water will cause dangerous spitting. Heat a large frying pan over the highest heat for 2 minutes until very hot. Add the olive oil and, when it shimmers, add the peppers in a single layer. Toss or stir frequently for 4 to 5 minutes until the skins are blistered, wrinkled, and dark in patches on all sides. The peppers should be soft when pressed. Tip onto a plate, scatter generously with sea salt flakes, and eat immediately. They wilt quickly.

items.fix: Dry the peppers thoroughly. Get the pan fully hot before adding the oil. Work in a single layer; overcrowding drops the temperature.
items.cause: Pan not hot enough, or peppers were wet
items.symptom: Peppers are steaming rather than blistering

items.fix: The peppers need 4 to 5 minutes total, not 2; toss constantly to blister all sides evenly
items.cause: Heat too high and cooked too quickly
items.symptom: Skin is charred but flesh is raw

content.text: Pimientos de Padrón became the most ordered tapa in Spain during the 1990s and spread from Galicia to every bar in the country. They are eaten with cold Albariño in Galicia or cold beer everywhere else, always finger food, always standing up, always with salt on the plate and a napkin somewhere nearby. The pepper roulette is the reason everyone reaches for another.
